Trivers 1971 — reciprocal altruism in a spatially-explicit ABM

Source: vignettes/paper-trivers1971.Rmd
paper-trivers1971.Rmd

Spatially-explicit clade implementation of Robert Trivers’ (1971) reciprocal altruism, using neighborhood encounters + per-agent partner memory to enable conditional cooperation. The headline finding is not that cooperation easily evolves; it is that cooperation is fragile in a spatial sim with mobile agents — which is consistent with the famously sparse empirical evidence for reciprocal altruism in nature (Stevens & Hauser 2004; Carter & Wilkinson 2013; Clutton-Brock 2009). clade lets us study this difficulty rather than gloss over it.

What Trivers proposed

Trivers (1971) gave the first formal evolutionary argument for how cooperation between non-kin could evolve. The mechanism is conditional: agents help others on the expectation of being helped back. Five conditions for evolutionary stability:

  1. Long lifespan — many opportunities to repeat.
  2. Low dispersal — partners encountered repeatedly.
  3. Mutual dependence — incentive to keep partners alive.
  4. Partner recognition — know who helped you.
  5. Cheater discrimination — withhold help from defectors.

Trivers’ paper is verbal-conceptual. Axelrod (1981, 1984) operationalised the mechanism as iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma with global random pairing, showed tit-for-tat (TFT) wins his tournaments, and triggered a vast literature on conditional cooperation strategies.

What the empirical evidence says (the puzzle)

Despite Trivers’ compelling argument, clear empirical examples of reciprocal altruism in nature are rare. The canonical case is vampire bats (Wilkinson 1984: blood-sharing among unrelated roost-mates) — and even that is contested (Carter & Wilkinson 2013 attribute much of the sharing to social bonds and food calls rather than strict TFT).

Reviews:

  • Stevens & Hauser (2004): cognitive constraints (memory, recognition, numerical reasoning) make TFT difficult for most non-human species.
  • Clutton-Brock (2009): most “altruism” in social mammals is better explained by kin selection or by-product mutualism than by reciprocity.
  • Hammerstein (2003): “the empirical record provides little support for reciprocal altruism as a major force in the evolution of cooperation.”

So the question becomes: why is reciprocal altruism so rare in nature when it should be evolutionarily favoured under Trivers’ conditions? Cognitive constraints are one answer. Another — which clade’s spatially-explicit ABM lets us examine directly — is that even granting all the cognitive machinery, the spatial structure of real animal encounters makes TFT-style reciprocity hard to evolve.

The clade implementation

clade is spatially explicit. Trivers’ theory is non-spatial (Axelrod’s operationalisation pairs randomly). We re-implement Trivers using clade’s spatial machinery:

  • Encounters: agents within Moore radius reciprocity_radius (default 1 = immediate neighbours) may interact each tick with probability reciprocity_interaction_rate (default 0.1).
  • Partner memory: each agent carries a ring buffer of size partner_memory_size (default 8) of (partner_id, last_action).
  • Strategy: generous tit-for-tat parameterised by three heritable traits in [0,1]: reciprocity_initial, reciprocity_retaliation, reciprocity_forgiveness.
  • Payoffs form a standard PD matrix scaled by reciprocity_cost and reciprocity_benefit_ratio. Cooperation transfers energy from actor to partner with multiplier b/c; defection takes energy.

See inst/julia/src/modules/reciprocity.jl for the implementation.

The headline (uncomfortable) result

At default parameters (reciprocity_cost = 0.5, b/c = 2, interaction_rate = 0.1, partner_memory_size = 8), cooperation collapses to defection in spatially-explicit clade simulations even though all five Trivers conditions are met. The mean reciprocity_initial trait drops below the no-reciprocity drift baseline. Defectors invade and the population either becomes predominantly defective or crashes.

library(clade)

s <- trivers_reciprocity_specs()   # max_age=500, dispersal=FALSE, default cost/b
s$max_ticks   <- 5000L
s$random_seed <- 42L
env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)
# At end: mean reciprocity_initial ≈ 0.22, n_alive ≈ 15. Cooperation lost.

The favourable regime

Cooperation can win, but only in a narrow parameter regime: low cost per cooperation and modest benefit ratio. This regime corresponds biologically to interactions with small individual cost (food calls, allogrooming, predator alarm calls) — exactly the contexts where empirical examples of reciprocal-altruism-like behaviour cluster.

s <- trivers_reciprocity_specs()
s$reciprocity_cost          <- 0.1     # small cost per game
s$reciprocity_benefit_ratio <- 2.0     # b/c = 2 (Hamilton's threshold)
s$max_ticks                 <- 2000L
s$random_seed               <- 42L
env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)
# At end: mean reciprocity_initial ≈ 0.72 (rose from 0.50 baseline).
# Cooperation evolves above no-reciprocity control.

The verified test at tests/testthat/test-reciprocal-altruism.R asserts only that cooperation rises by Δ > 0.05 with seed 42 in this favourable regime (typical observation: off ≈ 0.500 → on ≈ 0.718, Δ ≈ +0.218 — well above the 0.05 detection floor).

Dispersal-rate sweep

Trivers’ second condition (low dispersal) predicts cooperation should decline as dispersal rate rises. The clade test would be:

sweep_dispersal <- function(rate, seed = 42L) {
  s <- trivers_reciprocity_specs()
  s$reciprocity_cost          <- 0.1
  s$reciprocity_benefit_ratio <- 2.0
  s$max_ticks                 <- 2000L
  s$random_seed               <- seed
  s$dispersal_evolution       <- TRUE
  s$dispersal_init_mean       <- rate
  s$dispersal_mutation_sd     <- 0.0    # lock at this rate
  env <- run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE)
  recs  <- env$agents
  alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),
                  logical(1L))
  if (sum(alive) < 5L) return(NA_real_)
  mean(vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],
              function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$reciprocity_initial),
              numeric(1L)))
}

rates <- c(0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0)
init_means <- sapply(rates, sweep_dispersal)
plot(rates, init_means, type = "b", pch = 19,
     xlab = "Dispersal rate", ylab = "Mean reciprocity_initial",
     main = "Trivers dispersal-rate sweep — cooperation declines with mixing")
abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "grey")   # no-reciprocity baseline

Expected pattern: cooperation high at low dispersal (where partners re-encounter), declining toward 0.5 (drift baseline) or below as dispersal rises (mean-field-like mixing makes TFT useless).

Why the difficulty matters

clade’s findings — that spatially-explicit reciprocal altruism requires a narrow favourable regime to evolve, even with all of Trivers’ conditions — supports the empirical literature’s puzzle:

  • Real animals face the same spatial structure clade models. If an ABM with idealised cognition (perfect partner memory, perfect recognition, optimal TFT) struggles to evolve cooperation, it is no surprise that real animals with bounded cognition struggle even more.
  • The “narrow favourable regime” (low cost per game, modest benefit ratio) maps onto exactly the empirical examples that are sometimes argued to be reciprocity (food calls, allogrooming) — and away from the high-stakes contexts (food-sharing in famine, lethal injury risk in defence) where Trivers-style reciprocity might be most needed.
  • This is consistent with the “by-product mutualism” school (Brown 1983, Connor 1995): most apparent cooperation in nature is mutually beneficial in the moment, not Trivers-style delayed reciprocity.

What’s left for follow-up work

  • Cognitive-constraint variants — limit partner memory to 1, 2, 4 partners (Stevens & Hauser scale) and quantify how cooperation evolution degrades.
  • Partner choice instead of forced-pairing TFT — let agents choose whom to interact with based on memory; tests Noë & Hammerstein
    1. biological-market predictions.
  • Compare to direct/indirect reciprocity — Nowak & Sigmund (1998) on indirect reciprocity (reputation-mediated). clade could implement reputation as a separate trait.
  • Compare to kin selection — clade’s existing kin_selection module produces strong cooperation under high relatedness. Side-by- side comparison would quantify when each mechanism dominates.
  • Vampire-bat-specific scenario — long-lived agents, dense roost, low movement, food-sharing payoff matrix calibrated to Wilkinson’s data. Would clade reproduce vampire-bat-like cooperation in this specific regime?

References

  • Trivers, R.L. (1971) The evolution of reciprocal altruism. Quarterly Review of Biology 46:35–57.
  • Axelrod, R. & Hamilton, W.D. (1981) The evolution of cooperation. Science 211:1390–1396.
  • Wilkinson, G.S. (1984) Reciprocal food sharing in the vampire bat. Nature 308:181–184.
  • Stevens, J.R. & Hauser, M.D. (2004) Why be nice? Psychological constraints on the evolution of cooperation. Trends in Cognitive Sciences 8:60–65.
  • Clutton-Brock, T. (2009) Cooperation between non-kin in animal societies. Nature 462:51–57.
  • Carter, G.G. & Wilkinson, G.S. (2013) Food sharing in vampire bats: reciprocal help predicts donations more than relatedness or harassment. Proc. R. Soc. B 280:20122573.
  • Hammerstein, P. (2003) Genetic and Cultural Evolution of Cooperation. MIT Press, Cambridge MA.
  • Brown, J.L. (1983) Cooperation — a biologist’s dilemma. Adv. Stud. Behav. 13:1–37.
  • Nowak, M.A. & Sigmund, K. (1998) Evolution of indirect reciprocity by image scoring. Nature 393:573–577.
